issues_found: Source content is primarily education/labor economics (student loans, tuition, homeownership demographics) rather than financial market structure research
Record accurately captures the source but the source itself has low relevance to stated archive focus areas (market structure, macro catalysts)
Published July 2017 (8.75 years old), exceeding 90-day threshold; archival value assessment needed
The TARGET metadata ('market structure') does not match actual source content
human_review_reason: Source content is factually accurate but presents a scope mismatch. The research is well-documented and credible (Federal Reserve Bank of New York staff report), but its subject matter—education economics, student loan absorption, and homeownership demographics—falls outside typical financial market structure/macro catalyst focus. Human judgment needed to determine if housing market effects from student debt qualify as market structure context for this archive, and whether the 2017 publication date still has sufficient archival value given the long-term nature of the findings.

corrected_fields.macro_context: The research examines long-term demographic trends linking rising education costs to household formation and housing market participation. While not a traditional macro catalyst, it provides context on structural factors affecting housing demand and consumer spending patterns among young adults.
corrected_fields.market_structure_context: This research does not directly address financial market structure, trading mechanisms, or market infrastructure. It focuses on real economy dynamics—specifically how the student loan system affects household balance sheets and housing market participation.
